parts.text:
Here are three ways to rewrite that email. Each one addresses the delay and the deadline but adjusts the phrasing to focus on the **workflow** rather than a **personal reprimand.**

### Option 1: Action-oriented (Professional & Firm)
This version keeps it brief but frames it as a "check-in" rather than an accusation.
> "Hi [Name], I haven't received the report yet. Since we’ve missed the last few deadlines, I want to make sure we stay on track. Can you please have this finished and sent over by Friday?"

### Option 2: Collaborative (Softens the "Directness")
This version uses "we" language to make the problem about the project’s success rather than their personal failure.
> "Hi [Name], just following up on the report. We really need to get these in on schedule to keep the project moving. Could you please ensure it's delivered by this Friday?"

### Option 3: Short & Direct (The "Middle Ground")
If you want to stay as close to your original draft as possible while removing the "rude" edge, use this. It replaces the command "Fix it" with a request. 
> "Hi [Name], following up on the late report. This has been a recurring issue, so please make sure to have it to me by Friday so we don’t fall behind."

***

### Why these versions work better:
*   **Replacing "You didn't" with "I haven't received":** Starting with "You" often feels like a finger-point. Starting with the status of the document feels more objective.
*   **Replacing "This is a problem" with "To stay on track":** This explains *why* the delay matters without sounding like a lecture.
*   **Replacing "Fix it" with "Can you please/Please ensure":** In email-speak, "Fix it" functions as a command, which usually triggers defensiveness. Adding a "Please" or "Can you" maintains your authority but follows standard office etiquette.
